course_description:
This course is aimed at exposing engineering students to the innovation and development processes around medical devices. It aims at covering aspects in system engineering and design principles, medical device regulation, quality manufacturing systems, biomedical engineering ethics, and clinical validation. Students will also gain an appreciation on general working principles (engineering subsystems and associated biophysical processes) of selected medical devices across different risk categorization levels (Class-I-III), and current research trends.

This course will provide detailed coverage of the engineering aspects of medical devices. Key topics include:

Both current practice and research perspectives will be examined through case studies and applied workshops.

Invited lectures from relevant stakeholders (e.g. clinicians, patients, and industry representatives) are organised. These activities are designed to expose students to medical device engineering and medical devices “in practice”.

Students are expected to work on a team project and to conduct independent study and research to (i) prepare for scheduled sessions, and (ii) deliver assessment items in the form of written reports and oral presentations.

Changes in this offering (in response to student feedback and teaching-team reflection): In this offering, the course content and learning activities have been reorganised around an end-to-end medical device development workflow (need identification → intended use → requirements/design controls → risk management → verification/validation → clinical/ethics → regulatory strategy and translation). The team project has been updated with clearer scaffolding (templates, staged milestones, and marking rubrics) to improve consistency and transparency. Additional consultation opportunities with teaching staff have been added to support students’ progress.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
This course is designed to encourage live discussions during scheduled class time. Their format is designed for students to learn the most through interaction with the lecturer, demonstrators and colleagues during these live sessions. As such, students are highly encouraged to attend the 4-hours of scheduled classes each week.ﾠ
Irrespective of how they are labelled in the timetable (WKS), sessions will include a mix of lecture, workshop and discussion-style activities. It is expected that you attend each session ready and prepared to actively contribute to the class.
Invited lectures and Field trips will be organized during these scheduled sessions. Information about upcoming activities will be announced via Blackboard. Information will be provided well in advance to assist in scheduling, in case these activities fall outside scheduled classes.
UQ student policy expects that students spend 10-12 hours per week for each course to achieve a PASS grade. As such, students should plan to spend a
minimum
of 6-hrs of further self-study and research per week outside of scheduled class time. Students who want to achieve higher grades should anticipate spending much more time.
course_aims: This course is aimed at exposing engineering students to the innovation and development processes around medical devices. It aims at covering aspects in system engineering and design principles, medical device regulation, quality manufacturing systems, biomedical engineering ethics, and clinical validation. Students will also gain an appreciation onﾠgeneral working principles (engineering subsystems and associated biophysical processes) of selected medical devices across different risk categorization levels (Class-I-III), and current research trends.

learning_outcomes.description: Analyse and evaluate medical devices by identifying their medical need, value proposition, underlying medical condition, existing and emerging solutions, and operating principles, while assessing the technical basis of their functionality and subsystem integration.

Critically investigate and evaluate medical devices by appraising their intended purpose, efficacy & efficiency in treating or diagnosing medical conditions, technical sub-components, safety risks and limitations, ethical considerations, and research directions.

Apply specialized engineering knowledge and practices to ideate, design, prototype, and develop a medical device, including the identification of user needs, translation into technical requirements, validation and verification, application of relevant standards, and effective documentation practices to ensure compliance with Quality Management Systems.

Explain in detail, the innovation and product development cycle of medical devices, including considerations for compliance with regulatory agencies and international standards across different classifications.

Communicate effectively, both orally and in writing, the purpose, operation, technical functionality, regulatory considerations, and research directions of selected medical devices to technical, clinical, and lay audiences.
Demonstrate the ability to implement professional engineering principles while effectively collaborating in a multi-disciplinary team, showing respect for diverse ideas, work styles, and backgrounds.
Appraise and provide constructive feedback to peers’ work by employing a SPARK (Specific, Prescriptive, Actionable, Referenced and Kind) approach.

assessment_summary.title: 1. Structured Peer Feedback on Team Presentations

assessment_details.title: 1. Structured Peer Feedback on Team Presentations
assessment_details.task_description: Students will provide structured peer feedback on team presentations delivered in Week 5. Each student will submit feedback using the instructions that will be announced on Blackboard, focusing on: clarity of the clinical need and intended use logic from needs to requirements/claims identification of key risks and proposed controls quality of evidence/verification and validation thinking (as appropriate at this stage) communication effectiveness for mixed technical and clinical audiences Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may use AI and/or MT to complete this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Students need to submit the feedback form through the Blackboard assignment submission portal. It is the student's responsibility to get confirmation of report submission before closing the computer.
Further details about the form and submission process will be available on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extensions are limited to 7 days as students will need to receive feedback within 14 days in order to incorporate it for future submissions.
If you are unable to attend the presentation session, you will need to apply for an extension via my.UQ. If approved, an alternative peer-review task (e.g., review of a recorded presentation or slide deck) will be provided.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: 2. Peer Feedback on Submitted Reports
assessment_details.task_description: Students will provide structured peer feedback on a team’s submitted report in Week 8. Each student must review at least one team project report and submit a concise review that identifies strengths, risks/gaps, and improvement actions. Feedback must be professional, evidence-based, and aligned to course expectations, addressing: alignment between intended use, requirements, and proposed system architecture completeness and testability of requirements (acceptance criteria) risk file quality (hazard–harm–control logic, residual risk reasoning) verification/validation plan coherence and feasibility consideration of standards/regulatory pathway, ethics, and safety clarity and professionalism of technical writing and documentation Students must provide prioritized recommendations . Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may use AI and/or MT to complete this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: 3. Team Project
assessment_details.task_description: Team Project (40%) – Sequenced assessment (staged, marked deliverables) The team project evaluates students’ ability to work effectively in teams and apply biomedical engineering practice to develop a simple medical device concept while demonstrating evidence-based decision making across the medical device development workflow. Teams will capture and refine stakeholder needs, translate needs into measurable requirements, apply design control and risk management principles, and demonstrate consideration of relevant standards/regulatory pathways, safety, and ethics. The project begins from a predefined clinical need provided by a stakeholder (e.g., clinician, patient, industry). Teams will investigate the need and context of use, propose a device concept, and develop a documented development plan consistent with the frameworks covered in the course (e.g., needs finding and needs specification, design controls, risk management, verification/validation planning, and translation considerations). Milestones and weighting (within 40%) Milestone 1 – Identify needs and project plan (5%) (Week 5; in-class, identity verified) Teams deliver a 10-minute presentation (plus ~5 minutes Q&A) describing the clinical need, stakeholder analysis, value proposition, key assumptions/constraints, and an initial project plan. Teams are also assessed on initial documentation progress for the IDENTIFY phase (e.g., intended use/context, stakeholder map, initial risks). Milestone 2 – Design controls progress checkpoint (10%) (Week 8; submission) Teams submit a structured progress package demonstrating development of requirements and acceptance criteria, initial risk file (hazard–harm–controls), and an outline verification/validation plan, including OH&S and ethics considerations relevant to prototyping/testing. Feedback is provided to support improvement toward the final deliverables. Milestone 3 – Final deliverables and exhibition (25%) (Week 12; report submission + in-class presentation) Teams submit the final deliverables and present a short pitch during the project exhibition. Final deliverables include: A report summarising the clinical need, design process, key design decisions, and planned/undertaken verification/validation activities; Each student should write a section about their contribution in the project. Supporting documentation (e.g., requirements/specifications, risk management file, traceability, standards/regulatory considerations, ethics/safety, test plans/results as applicable); Students need to present their work with their team. Each student will present the part of the work they are responsible for and will be assessed individually. The presentation should give an overall overview of the proposal they worked on together. Each student will be expected to answer questions about their presentation. Guidelines and marking rubrics for the presentation will be available on Blackboard. A peer assessment factor is applied to moderate individual marks where contributions differ materially. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may use AI and/or MT to complete this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. In accordance with UQ Assessment Policy, your presentation will be recorded. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission instructions will be announced on the course Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If a student cannot attend a scheduled presentation due to approved circumstances, an alternative individual presentation/viva will be arranged.
Extensions for group work assessments may be available and will require a single request submitted with agreement from at least 50% of the group members and recognition of potential impacts on the other group members. Student Access Plans for an individual student do not guarantee an extension for the assessment item.
Late submission
Milestone 2
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Milestone 1 & 3
Consistent with industry practice around presentations to clients/industry partners, the 100% late penalty applies.
This has been approved by the Associate Dean (Academic).

assessment_details.title: 4. Final Exam
assessment_details.task_description: A case-based examination assessing individual understanding of medical device engineering and translation. Students will analyse a device scenario and respond to applied questions covering intended use and risk classification reasoning, requirements and design controls, risk management (hazards, harms, controls, residual risk), verification and validation planning, and regulatory/standards strategy. The exam includes short-answer and applied scenario questions. The exam will assess students for the following: Device definition and classification thinking Needs → requirements → evidence logic Risk management and controls Verification/validation and clinical evaluation planning Software/AI and data considerations (where relevant) This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 120 minutes Calculator options (In person) Casio FX82 series only or UQ approved and labelled calculator Open/closed book Closed book examination - specified written materials permitted Materials One A4 sheet of typed notes, double sided, is permitted Exam platform Paper based Invigilation Invigilated in person
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Studio There are 4 hours of scheduled activities in this course. Irrespective of how they are labelled in the timetable, these sessions will include a mix of lecture, studio and discussion-style activities. These sessions will include presentations of the course material and discussions, with active student participation encouraged. Background material will be provided by the lecturer. Invited lecturers/seminars are also planned. Learning outcomes: L03, L04

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Active learning Students will be pointed to recommended articles, book chapters, and material that are relevant to the course's learning objectives. It is expected that students spend a good amount of time studying the recommended material. Students will be expected to do short presentations during scheduled class, and participate in discussion forums based on the recommended material. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 5 (09 Mar - 29 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Invited talks Medical devices engineering is a very comprehensive and trans-disciplinary field. To give students the opportunity to learn from different stakeholders (e.g. clinicians, patients, industry representatives), invited lectures / seminars will be organized throughout the semester. These will happen during scheduled class hours, unless otherwise announced. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 12 (09 Mar - 24 May)
learning_activities.topic: Team project Students will work on the conceptual design of a medical device based on a real clinical need. Students will apply medical device engineering practices learned throughout the course. Students will work in teams of 3-4 members. A major assessment component of this course is related to this activity. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 8 (09 Mar - 26 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Field excursions One or two field excursions, within short reach of UQ (e.g. Metro North Health facilities), are planned within scheduled hours. Once confirmed, a detailed schedule of activities will be available in Blackboard. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
